response.explanation:
Continuing Airworthiness Management Responsibilities

The continuing airworthiness management system (CAMO) must clearly define who is responsible for each part of the process, how those responsibilities are exercised, and how the system is controlled, especially when tasks are subcontracted. The key responsibilities are grouped into several categories:

Category What the CAMO must do Who is responsible
Nominated Postholder • Ensure all maintenance is performed on time and to an approved standard.
• Hold corporate authority to finance and carry out all continuing‑airworthiness activities.
• Have the authority required by Part‑M to enforce standards.		 The nominated postholder for continuing airworthiness (M.A.706(d)).
Group of Persons • Cover all responsibilities described in Part‑M.
• Provide detailed job functions that constitute the group.		 The “group of persons” required by M.A.706(c). In small operators this may be the same as the nominated postholder.
Manpower & Training • Show that the number of people assigned to the CAMO is adequate (provide broad figures, not the whole company).
• Demonstrate a training policy that ensures all personnel are qualified.		 All management personnel involved in continuing airworthiness.
Records & Logbooks • Manage the aircraft’s continuing‑airworthiness records and technical log.
• Record flight hours and cycles accurately for maintenance planning.		 The CAMO’s records‑management team.
Minimum Equipment List (MEL) • Issue, update, use, and manage the MEL (if applicable). The CAMO.
Maintenance Programme • Identify State‑of‑Registry requirements.
• Develop, update, approve, or accept the maintenance programme.
• Identify sources for the programme and mandatory tasks.		 The CAMO.
Subcontracting • Approve and control subcontracted continuing‑airworthiness tasks.
• Include procedures in the CAMO exposition that reflect control of subcontract arrangements.
• Require a contract that:
 – Specifies the subcontractor’s responsibilities.
 – Requires the subcontractor to inform the CAMO (and the competent authority) of any changes that affect their ability to fulfil the contract.
 – Provides for competent‑authority monitoring and closure of findings.
 – Gives the CAMO access to all relevant data and the right to override recommendations if necessary.
 – Ensures the subcontractor has qualified technical expertise and sufficient resources.		 The CAMO (for oversight) and the subcontracted organisation (for execution).

Key Points to Remember

  1. Authority & Oversight – The nominated postholder must have the corporate authority to finance and enforce all continuing‑airworthiness tasks.
  2. Coverage of Responsibilities – The group of persons must cover every duty outlined in Part‑M; for small operators this group may be the same as the nominated postholder.
  3. Manpower & Training – Provide evidence that the CAMO has enough trained staff; detailed employee counts for the whole company are unnecessary.
  4. Records Management – Accurate recording of flight hours and cycles is essential for maintenance planning.
  5. MEL & Maintenance Programme – The CAMO must issue, update, and manage the MEL and the maintenance programme in line with state requirements.
  6. Subcontracting Controls – All subcontracted tasks must be governed by clear procedures, contracts, and monitoring to ensure compliance and maintain CAMO approval.

These responsibilities collectively ensure that the aircraft remains airworthy, that maintenance is performed correctly, and that the CAMO retains control over all activities, whether performed in‑house or by subcontractors.
